video.title: Crisol: Theater of Idols | Fully Ramblomatic

captions.text: Crisol: Theatre of Idols might sound like a—bear  with me on this—a reality TV show for juggalos
sponsored by a brand of inexpensive floor  cleaner, but it's actually a new, original,
first-person survival horror that caught my  interest. Try to imagine if Bioshock had a child
with Resident Evil 8 that was frequently babysat  by Lies of P and who spent a lot of time hanging
out with Blasphemous during their awkward, defiant  teenage years. Of the several games I tried last
week, it was the one I ended up sticking with  because I was drawn to the main character, who
is an absolute prize twat. Game recognizes game, I  suppose. In an alternative mid-20th-century Spain,
we play as Gabriel, the chosen warrior of the  world's mainstream sun-worshipping religion,
who is sent by his god to the island of a  sea-worshipping cult to murder their god
and evangelize with both barrels. And early  on, I was engaged by this setting because,
irreligious as I am, sun worship has always  made a lot of sense to me. At least we know
that the sun exists. It's a great counter to  smug atheist questions. "Where is your god?"
"He's up there. Where I'm pointing. Keeping us  all alive. Any further questions, fuckface?"
And we're up against sea worshippers, and that  makes a lot of sense as well. The sea is also a
thing that exists. Which is very, very big and  mysterious and which provides us nourishment,
but if we don't show it enough respect, it  fucking kills us with sharks. It's an immediately
understandable world and core conflict, and while  Gabriel himself is in large part standard, gruff,
prickly antihero soldier man, he's also  a screamingly devout religious nutter,
which is the little bit of extra oomph that makes  a character fun to be around—as a game protagonist
if not as a party guest. Having said that, Crisol  is by a Spanish developer and often gives me
that Atomic Heart vibe where the characters and  dialogue feel a bit off because certain nuances
have gotten lost in translation. For example,  there's the main female support NPC, who I think
is supposed to be bubbly and facetious but more  comes across like she's on the verge of a nervous
breakdown in every dialogue line. Anyway, let's  talk core gameplay loops. Yeah, I know you love
those. Gabriel must gather four artefacts from  four prominent members of the local community,
exploring the city and learning the backstory  behind everyone getting killed by a horde of
demonic puppets and a big, evil, unkillable,  metal lady with a very inconsistent accent.
To combat them, Gabriel must adopt the guise  of his superhero alter ego, the Amazing Captain
Exertion Grunt. It made me laugh how much of this  game's soundtrack is just Gabriel going, "NGH URGH
NGH RGH" like a seal falling down the stairs.  Even a, like, one-foot drop provokes a noise
like a fat guy trying to get up from a beanbag  chair. The combat has your standard Resident Evil
4 suite of guns—pistol, shotgun, rifle, SMG—and  also a very Resident Evil 4 Remake-y knife with
a parry and a degradation system, but before you  hurl your monitor through the head of the nearest
cat in response to that last point, I ended the  game with, like, 16 unused knife restoration items
because I was using the knife very infrequently;  if any of the tottering wooden puppet monsters
get close enough to do anything that might warrant  a parry, then you've already fucked up. It's not
a question of conserving ammo because there is  no ammo. This is the front-facing core gameplay
gimmick. Instead, your guns all run on blood. Your  blood, specifically, before you all go running to
the menstrual product disposal bin. Each gun  has one or more vicious spikes sticking out
of where ammo would normally go, and Gabriel has  to mash his hand on them to refill the bullets.
So, you know what that means: even reloading  provokes an exertion grunt sound. The recording
sessions for this dude must've sounded like taking  a horny chimp to the gym. And in practical terms,
it means you sacrifice your health bar to refill  your ammo. Which, philosophically, does sound like
a fun idea for a horror game—a constant dilemma  between doing damage and defending against it—but
in practice, it means all of the game mechanics  revolve around a single resource. And we end up in
a binary state system. You've either got lots of  the resource, in which case you're fine, or you've
got little, in which case you're buggered. And  health for reloads brings with it universal ammo,
which has never been a good idea. It means you can  use whatever gun you want at any given moment, and
it'll always have shots as long as you can squeeze  out another drop of claret for it. In short,
it makes for a much shallower survival experience  than if resources were spread around a bit. And
rather undercuts the thrill of discovery. Boy, I  wonder what's in this crate. Oh. A blood syringe.
Like the last seven crates. I really thought  this time it'd be basmati rice or something.
It's funny, though; I should have harsher feelings  about a game with such a glaring flaw in its core
gameplay, but I don't. In fact, I rather enjoyed  our time, mainly on the strength of atmosphere,
story, and creative visuals. The wooden  puppet monsters are effectively creepy
and nicely designed, which is just as well, as  you fucking see a lot of them. Some more combat
variety wouldn't have gone amiss—there's only two  insultingly easy boss fights right at the end,
both of whom telegraph their attacks like  they're helping you back into a parking
space—at all previous times, when you're about  to confront the villain of the current area,
they have an alarming tendency to kill themselves  right before you reach them, which just goes to
show what boring company Gabriel must be. There's  the odd annoying forced stealth bit with the giant
metal lady and some obnoxious sliding tile puzzles  on the critical path that I found to be a bit of
a roadblock when I was more in the mindset of  "shoot anything that moves and isn't a friendly
dog," but on the whole, just the general sense of  effort put into the world and its environs carried
me through to a borderline positive. I did,  however, sour a bit more on it at the very end,
but I'm saving that for after the spoiler warning.  Which I believe is coming up now. Yes, here it is.
SPOILER SPOILER SPOILER SPOILER WARNING WARNING  WARNING WARNING. IF YOU DON'T WANT ENDING SPOILERS
SPOILERS SPOILERS SPOILERS SPOILERS SPOILERS, THEN  PISS OFF. It all comes down to a last-minute plot
twist in that it turns out Gabriel's not just  the chosen warrior of God, he IS God. Having
taken human form out of penance for what he did  to the villains long ago. And my first reaction
to that reveal was "Oh! ... so what?" That doesn't  actually change anything or pay off any previous
event, just means we go from right hand chosen  warrior of God to cutting out the middleman,
as it were. Smacks of the David Cage-brand twist  for the sake of having a twist. If anything,
it removes a potentially interesting conflict  because Gabriel's arc throughout the game has
been about coming to question the righteousness  of his god, and there's a brewing sense that
some drama will ensue when he gets home and  has a few difficult questions for the big,
fiery bastard, but now it transpires he IS the  big, fiery bastard, that conversation's gonna
get a hell of a lot easier. "Hey, I think  we might be in the wrong. That's funny,
I was just thinking the same thing. Well, glad we  sorted that out. Thanks for bringing it up, me."
